title: DHB Token Explained + How to Buy DeHub in the UK (Step-by-Step)
html:
Don't invest unless you're prepared to lose all the money you invest. This is a high-risk investment and you should not expect to be protected if something goes wrong. Take 2 mins to learn more.

If you want to know how to buy DHB token in the UK, the short version is this: set up a self-custody crypto wallet, buy ETH or USDC on an FCA-registered exchange that serves UK customers, move those funds onto the Base network, and swap them for $DHB on a decentralised exchange. DHB is the ecosystem token of DeHub, a Web3 social network, and it runs primarily on Base with contracts also live on BNB Chain and Polygon. This guide walks through each step carefully and explains what the token actually does, so you can decide whether it belongs in your plans at all.

This article is information, not financial advice. Nothing here is a recommendation to buy, sell or hold any asset. Cryptoassets are unregulated and high-risk in the UK; always do your own research and, if in doubt, speak to an independent, FCA-authorised financial adviser.

What is the DHB token?

$DHB is the native utility token of DeHub, the decentralised social network built by DeLabs Ltd, a United Kingdom company. DeHub is an open-source, user-owned, censorship-resistant social platform for creators and communities, and DHB is the unit that powers the economic layer sitting underneath it.

Unlike a token that exists only to be traded, DHB has jobs to do inside a live product. The point of buying it, for most people, is to use it, not simply to hold it.

What DHB is used for

Across the DeHub ecosystem, the token is woven into how the platform actually works:

  • Pay-per-view unlocks and token-gating. Creators can tokenise an upload so it is free, pay-per-view, or gated behind a token requirement. DHB is the currency that unlocks that content.
  • Tokenised subscriptions. DeHub offers on-chain, tradable subscriptions that a fan can later resell, with resale royalties flowing back to the creator. If you are new to the concept, our guide to DeHub tokenised subscriptions breaks down how they differ from an ordinary monthly plan.
  • Tips and live-stream support. On-chain live streams carry animated tips, and DHB is one way viewers back the creators they rate in real time.
  • Watch-to-earn and gaming rewards. DeHub runs watch-to-earn bounties and a play-to-earn arcade, including the flagship MMA battle-royale game Last Chad Standing. The token flows through those reward loops.
  • Staking and ecosystem participation. DHB can be staked to take part in the ecosystem rather than sitting idle.

That utility is the reason people describe DeHub crypto as a product-first token: the demand comes from things you can do, not just from speculation.

DeHub tokenomics and which chains DHB runs on

On DeHub tokenomics, the honest and important point is that we will not quote you a price, a market cap, or a projected return anywhere in this guide. Those numbers move constantly, and no one can promise you what any cryptoasset will do next. What we can tell you is where the token lives and how to read reliable data on it.

DHB is a multi-chain token. Its primary home is Base, an Ethereum Layer 2 built by Coinbase, which keeps transaction fees low and settlement fast. There are also DHB contracts on BNB Chain and Polygon, so you have more than one network to work with depending on where your funds already sit.

For neutral market data, DHB is listed on both CoinGecko and CoinMarketCap. Those are the sensible places to check the live contract address, the networks a token is deployed on, and which venues it trades on before you move any money. Always copy the official contract address from a source you trust, because look-alike tokens with copied names are a common scam across every chain.

Where DHB trades

DHB trades in two broad places:

  • On decentralised exchanges (DEXs) on Base, BNB Chain and Polygon, where you swap directly from your own wallet.
  • On Coinbase, which matters for UK buyers because it is a well-known, UK-friendly on-ramp for converting pounds into the crypto you will need first.

If you want to see how DeHub's approach compares with other reward-driven platforms before committing, the round-up of the best watch-to-earn platforms in 2026 is a useful sense-check on where the ecosystem sits.

A quick word before you buy: the UK rules

In the UK, cryptoassets are largely unregulated, and buying them is legal. Since 2023 the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) has required firms that market cryptoassets to UK consumers to display a standard risk warning, add a cooling-off period for new customers, and drop "refer a friend" bonuses. That is why you now see the warning at the top of this page and why FCA-registered exchanges make you tick through risk prompts before your first purchase.

None of that makes crypto safe. It means the promotion is regulated, not the asset. If something goes wrong, you are unlikely to be protected by the Financial Services Compensation Scheme or the Financial Ombudsman Service. Treat any amount you put in as money you can afford to lose entirely.

How to buy DHB token in the UK: step-by-step

Here is a careful, general walk-through of how to buy DHB token as a UK resident. This is a description of the mechanics, not a recommendation to proceed. Take your time with each step and never rush a transaction.

Step 1 — Set up a self-custody wallet

You will need a Web3 wallet that supports Base, such as a reputable browser or mobile wallet. Self-custody means you control the private keys, and therefore you alone are responsible for security. Write your recovery phrase down on paper, store it offline, and never type it into a website, a chat, or a "support" agent. No legitimate service will ever ask for it.

Step 2 — Buy ETH or USDC on an FCA-registered exchange

You cannot usually buy DHB directly with pounds. Instead, use a UK-friendly, FCA-registered exchange, Coinbase is the obvious example given its Base connection, to convert GBP into a base currency you can spend on-chain. For Base, the practical choices are ETH (you will need a little for network fees regardless) or USDC. Complete the exchange's identity checks and clear the mandatory risk warnings and cooling-off period as a new customer.

Step 3 — Withdraw to your wallet on Base

Withdraw your ETH or USDC from the exchange to your own wallet address, choosing the Base network when prompted. Some exchanges, including Coinbase, let you withdraw straight onto Base, which saves a bridging step. Always send a tiny test amount first, confirm it arrives, and only then move the rest. Double-check the network every single time; sending to the wrong chain is one of the most common ways people lose funds.

Step 4 — Bridge to Base if you need to

If your funds landed on a different network, for example Ethereum mainnet, use a reputable bridge to move them to Base. If you already withdrew directly onto Base in step 3, skip this entirely. Keep a small amount of ETH on Base to cover the gas fees for your swap.

Step 5 — Swap for DHB on a DEX

Connect your wallet to a decentralised exchange on Base, paste the official DHB contract address taken from CoinGecko or CoinMarketCap (do not trust a random search result), and confirm the token before swapping. Set a sensible slippage tolerance, review the transaction details, and approve. Once the swap confirms, DHB will appear in your wallet, and you can start using it across DeHub.

Step 6 — Put it to use

Head to the DeHub app or grab it on Google Play and connect the same wallet. From there your DHB can unlock content, back creators, join watch-to-earn bounties, or play the arcade, which is the whole reason the token exists.

Staying safe and staying on the right side of tax

Two practical reminders that catch people out:

  • Security is on you. Bookmark the real site, https://dehub.io, verify contract addresses independently, and be sceptical of anyone in your DMs offering "help", giveaways, or guaranteed returns. Those are scams, without exception.
  • Tax may apply. In the UK, HMRC generally treats disposals of cryptoassets, including swapping one token for another, as potentially subject to Capital Gains Tax, and some crypto income can fall under Income Tax. Rules and allowances change, so check current HMRC guidance or ask a qualified accountant about your own position.

One more housekeeping note for anyone who arrived here by accident: the DeHub we are describing is the Web3 social network at dehub.io. It has nothing to do with DePaul University's "DeHUB" student portal or Rowan University's "DEHub" engineering hub. If you landed on the wrong one, our DeHub name disambiguation guide clears it up.

Frequently asked questions

Is DHB available on Coinbase?

DHB trades on Coinbase and across decentralised exchanges, and it is listed for market data on CoinGecko and CoinMarketCap. For UK buyers, Coinbase is also a convenient FCA-registered on-ramp for turning pounds into the ETH or USDC you need before swapping for $DHB on Base. Always confirm the official token and contract address from CoinGecko or CoinMarketCap before you trade.

What chain is the DHB token on?

DHB's primary chain is Base, an Ethereum Layer 2. There are also DHB contracts on BNB Chain and Polygon, so you can choose the network that best fits where your funds already sit. Whichever you pick, keep a small amount of the network's native gas token to cover transaction fees.

Is buying DHB safe?

No cryptoasset is "safe" in the sense of being protected or guaranteed. Cryptoassets are high-risk and largely unregulated in the UK, and you should be prepared to lose everything you put in. You can reduce avoidable risks by using self-custody wallets responsibly, verifying contract addresses, and ignoring anyone promising returns, but the market risk itself never goes away.

Is crypto legal in the UK?

Yes. Buying, holding and trading cryptoassets is legal in the UK. Since 2023 the FCA has regulated how crypto is marketed to UK consumers, which is why exchanges show mandatory risk warnings and cooling-off periods. Legal does not mean protected: if something goes wrong you generally cannot rely on the Financial Services Compensation Scheme or the Financial Ombudsman Service, and any gains may be taxable.

---

This guide is for information only and is not financial advice. Cryptoassets are high-risk and unregulated in the UK. Do your own research and consider independent, FCA-authorised advice before making any decision. Learn more about the wider ecosystem at DeHub, the decentralised social network or explore how it all fits together on the DeHub app.
